paths.title: IUI With Partner Sperm
paths.description: Intrauterine insemination using partner sperm
paths.suitable_for: Unexplained infertility, mild sperm factor, ovulatory dysfunction, or cervical factor

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and outline the IUI process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess reproductive health and determine the most appropriate approach. Testing may include bloodwork, imaging, semen analysis, and infectious disease screening.

A treatment plan is developed based on the test results. This includes deciding between a natural cycle (monitoring ovulation without medication) or a medicated cycle (using ovulation induction medications to support follicle development). A baseline assessment confirms readiness to begin.

The clinic monitors the cycle through regular appointments, including ultrasounds and bloodwork, to track follicle development and determine the optimal timing for insemination. If medications are being used, a trigger shot may be given when follicles reach the target size to time ovulation precisely.

What to be aware of: If medications are being used, the response may vary - too few or too many follicles may develop. In some cases, the cycle may be adjusted or cancelled if the response is not within a safe range. The clinical team makes this decision based on monitoring results.

On the day of the procedure, a fresh sperm sample is collected and prepared in the laboratory through a washing and concentration process that separates the most motile sperm from the seminal fluid. The prepared sperm are then placed directly into the uterus through a thin catheter - a brief procedure that most patients tolerate well with minimal discomfort. This bypasses the cervix, giving the sperm a shorter path to the fallopian tube where fertilization occurs.

Approximately two weeks after the IUI, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Based on the results, the next steps are discussed - either continuing with additional treatment cycles or transitioning to prenatal care if pregnancy is confirmed.

What to be aware of: IUI has a lower chance of success per cycle compared to IVF. It is common to undergo multiple IUI cycles before achieving pregnancy. If pregnancy has not occurred after several cycles, the clinical team will discuss whether to continue with IUI or consider other treatment options such as IVF.

paths.title: IUI With Donor Sperm
paths.description: Intrauterine insemination using donor sperm
paths.suitable_for: Single individuals, same-sex couples, severe sperm factor infertility, or when partner sperm is not available

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and outline the IUI with donor sperm process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess reproductive health and determine the most appropriate approach.

A sperm donor is selected from a donor bank. Profiles typically include information about physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor sperm.

A treatment plan is developed based on test results, including the decision between a natural cycle (monitoring ovulation without medication) or a medicated cycle (using ovulation induction medications).

The clinic monitors the cycle through regular appointments, including ultrasounds and bloodwork, to track follicle development and determine optimal timing for insemination. If medications are being used, a trigger shot may be given when follicles reach the target size.

What to be aware of: If medications are being used, the response may vary - too few or too many follicles may develop. In some cases, the cycle may be adjusted or cancelled if the response is not within a safe range. The clinical team makes this decision based on monitoring results.

On the day of the procedure, donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory through a washing and concentration process that separates the most motile sperm. The prepared sperm are placed directly into the uterus through a thin catheter - a brief procedure that most patients tolerate well with minimal discomfort.

What to be aware of: Frozen donor sperm may have reduced motility after thawing compared to a fresh sample. The laboratory accounts for this during preparation, and donor samples are selected to ensure adequate post-thaw quality for insemination.

Approximately two weeks after the IUI, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Based on the results, the next steps are discussed - either continuing with additional treatment cycles or transitioning to prenatal care.

What to be aware of: IUI has a lower chance of success per cycle compared to IVF. It is common to undergo multiple IUI cycles before achieving pregnancy. If pregnancy has not occurred after several cycles, the clinical team will discuss whether to continue with IUI or consider other treatment options such as IVF.

paths.title: IVF With Own Eggs & Partner Sperm
paths.description: In vitro fertilization using own eggs and partner sperm. The fertilization method (conventional IVF or ICSI) is determined by the clinical team based on sperm parameters and other factors.
paths.suitable_for: Tubal factor, endometriosis, sperm factor infertility, failed IUI cycles, unexplained infertility, or other indications for IVF

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and explain the IVF process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess reproductive health and guide treatment planning. Testing may include bloodwork, imaging, semen analysis, and infectious disease screening.

The clinical team selects the most appropriate medication protocol based on age, ovarian reserve, and individual factors. A baseline ultrasound and bloodwork are completed to confirm readiness to begin the stimulation cycle.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound to track follicle growth and bloodwork to measure hormone levels. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.

When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later. The timing of this injection is critical and must be followed exactly as directed by the clinical team.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes. A recovery period of 1 to 2 hours follows before discharge. Mild cramping afterward is normal.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

A sperm sample is collected and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized using either conventional insemination (placing eggs and sperm together in a dish) or ICSI (injecting a single sperm directly into each egg). The method is determined by the clinical team. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs have fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process. In uncommon cases, fertilization may not occur, and the clinical team will discuss possible reasons and next steps.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality to help identify the embryos with the strongest developmental potential. Genetic testing (PGT) may also be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone supplementation begins around this time (or at retrieval, depending on the protocol) to support the uterine lining. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen (vitrified) for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.
topic_refs: Fresh Embryo Transfer - Day Five (Blastocyst Stage)

steps.overview:
Approximately 9 to 14 days after embryo transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone support continues during this waiting period and, if pregnancy is confirmed, typically continues through the first trimester. Early monitoring ultrasounds are performed before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: IVF With Personal Eggs & Donor Sperm
paths.description: In vitro fertilization using own eggs and donor sperm
paths.suitable_for: Single individuals, same-sex couples, severe sperm factor infertility, absence of a sperm-providing partner, or genetic concerns requiring donor sperm

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and explain the IVF with donor sperm process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess reproductive health and guide treatment planning.

Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. These steps ensure all parties understand the treatment process, the risks involved, and the legal and ethical aspects of using donor gametes.

A sperm donor is selected from a donor bank. Profiles typically include information about physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor sperm.

The clinical team selects the most appropriate medication protocol based on age, ovarian reserve, and individual factors. A baseline ultrasound and bloodwork are completed to confirm readiness to begin. Instructions are provided for administering the fertility medications.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound to track follicle growth and bloodwork to measure hormone levels. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.

When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later. The timing of this injection is critical.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes. A recovery period of 1 to 2 hours follows before discharge.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized using either conventional insemination or ICSI, depending on the post-thaw sperm quality and the clinical team's recommendation. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs have fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Frozen donor sperm may have reduced motility after thawing, which the laboratory accounts for when selecting the fertilization method. Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process. In uncommon cases, fertilization may not occur, and the clinical team will discuss possible reasons and next steps.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality to help identify the embryos with the strongest developmental potential. Genetic testing (PGT) may also be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone supplementation begins around this time to support the uterine lining. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.
topic_refs: Fresh Embryo Transfer - Day Five (Blastocyst Stage)

steps.overview:
Approximately 9 to 14 days after embryo transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone support continues during this waiting period and, if pregnancy is confirmed, typically continues through the first trimester. Early monitoring ultrasounds are performed before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: IVF With Donor Eggs
paths.description: In vitro fertilization using donor eggs. ICSI is typically required when using frozen donor eggs due to changes in the egg shell during the freezing process.
paths.suitable_for: Advanced reproductive age, diminished ovarian reserve, poor egg quality, premature ovarian insufficiency, genetic concerns, or repeated IVF failure with own eggs

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and explain the donor egg IVF process. Required testing is completed to assess overall health and uterine receptivity for carrying a pregnancy.

A donor is selected from an egg bank or donor agency. Profiles typically include information about physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor eggs.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness is monitored by ultrasound. If using a fresh directed donor, this preparation is coordinated with the donor's stimulation cycle. If using frozen eggs from an egg bank, preparation proceeds on an independent timeline.

Sperm is collected from the partner or donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. Donor eggs are fertilized - typically using ICSI for frozen eggs, or either conventional insemination or ICSI for fresh eggs depending on the clinical situation. The following day, the embryology team checks fertilization results. The embryos are then cultured for 3 to 6 days, with the team monitoring development and grading quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: Not all frozen eggs will survive the thawing process, and not all surviving eggs will fertilize. Of those that fertilize, some embryos may stop developing during the culture period. This progressive attrition is a normal part of the process. The number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number of eggs that were thawed.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.
topic_refs: Fresh Embryo Transfer - Day Five (Blastocyst Stage)

steps.overview:
Approximately two weeks after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. If positive, progesterone and estrogen support continue and early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care. If negative, the clinical team discusses next steps, which may include transferring a frozen embryo from this cycle or planning a new cycle.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Double Donation IVF
paths.description: In vitro fertilization using both donor eggs and donor sperm. ICSI is typically used for fertilization when frozen donor eggs are involved.
paths.suitable_for: Single individuals, same-sex couples, situations where both egg and sperm donors are needed, or genetic concerns requiring dual donor screening

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and explain the double donation IVF process. Required testing is completed to assess overall health and readiness to carry a pregnancy.
Required infectious disease screening is completed and legal contracts are established to define parental rights. The clinic coordinates the preparation of donor eggs and donor sperm for the cycle.

Both an egg donor and a sperm donor are selected. Profiles typically include physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background. Genetic compatibility screening may be performed between the two donors to reduce the risk of passing inherited conditions to offspring. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor gametes.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness is monitored by ultrasound. This preparation occurs while the donor eggs and sperm are being coordinated for fertilization.

Donor eggs are thawed and donor sperm is prepared in the laboratory. ICSI is used to fertilize the eggs. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs survived thawing and fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all frozen eggs will survive the thawing process, and not all surviving eggs will fertilize. This is a normal part of the process.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality to identify the embryos with the strongest developmental potential. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.

Progesterone and any other prescribed medications continue during the waiting period to support potential implantation. Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. If positive, medications continue and early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care. If negative, the clinical team discusses next steps.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Reciprocal IVF
paths.description: A form of IVF in which one partner provides the eggs and the other carries the pregnancy. Donor sperm is used for fertilization. Embryos may be transferred fresh (with cycle synchronization) or frozen for transfer in a subsequent cycle.
paths.suitable_for: Partners who both wish to have a biological role in the pregnancy - one as the egg provider and one as the gestational carrier

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history for both partners, discuss treatment goals, and explain the reciprocal IVF process. Required fertility testing is completed for both the egg-providing partner and the carrying partner.

The clinical team selects the appropriate stimulation protocol for the egg-providing partner and plans the cycle coordination. If a fresh transfer is planned, the carrying partner's endometrial preparation is synchronized with the egg provider's stimulation. If a freeze-all approach is used, the cycles proceed independently. Baseline ultrasound and bloodwork confirm readiness to begin.

The egg-providing partner takes daily gonadotropin injections to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound and bloodwork. If the cycles are synchronized, the carrying partner begins endometrial preparation with estrogen during this time.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.

When the egg-providing partner's follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the egg-providing partner's ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized using either conventional insemination or ICSI, depending on the clinical situation. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs have fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Frozen donor sperm may have reduced motility after thawing, which the laboratory accounts for when selecting the fertilization method. Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated. If a synchronized fresh transfer is planned, the carrying partner continues endometrial preparation during this time.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the carrying partner's uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle.
topic_refs: Fresh Embryo Transfer - Day Five (Blastocyst Stage)

steps.overview:
Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, the carrying partner has a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG). Progesterone support continues during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care. If negative, the clinical team discusses next steps.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult for both partners. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: IVF With Gestational Carrier (Surrogacy)
paths.description: IVF in which embryos are created using own eggs (or donor eggs) and partner or donor sperm, then transferred to a gestational carrier who carries the pregnancy.
paths.suitable_for: Absence of a uterus (congenital or surgical), medical conditions that make pregnancy unsafe, recurrent pregnancy loss despite healthy embryos, or individuals who require a gestational carrier

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss treatment goals, and explain the gestational carrier IVF process. Required testing is completed for both the intended parent(s) and the gestational carrier.

Required infectious disease screening is completed for all parties. Comprehensive legal contracts are established to define parental rights, financial arrangements, and expectations. The gestational carrier completes medical and psychological screening.

The clinical team selects the appropriate stimulation protocol for the egg provider. Baseline ultrasound and bloodwork confirm readiness to begin. Instructions for administering fertility medications are provided.

The egg provider takes daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound and bloodwork. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.
When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Sperm is collected from the partner or donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized using conventional insemination or ICSI, depending on the clinical situation. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs have fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process. In uncommon cases, fertilization may not occur, and the clinical team will discuss possible reasons and next steps.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated. The gestational carrier begins or continues endometrial preparation with medications during this time.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the gestational carrier's uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. The gestational carrier continues progesterone support after transfer. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle.
topic_refs: Fresh Embryo Transfer - Day Five (Blastocyst Stage)

steps.overview:
Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, the gestational carrier has a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG). Progesterone support continues during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled and the gestational carrier transitions to prenatal care with appropriate specialist oversight.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Sperm Freezing
paths.description: Preserve fertility by freezing sperm for future use
paths.suitable_for: Before cancer treatment, before gonadotoxic medications, before vasectomy, before gender-affirming hormone therapy, or when future sperm availability may be uncertain

steps.overview: A referral to a cryopreservation facility is typically the first step. Referral requirements vary by region - in some cases a referral from a family doctor is sufficient, while other regions may require a specialist referral. The clinic will provide guidance on the local process.
Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the freezing procedure, storage terms, and future use of the stored samples.

A semen sample is collected, typically by ejaculation in a private collection room at the clinic. Some clinics allow home collection with specific transport and timing requirements. If ejaculation is not possible, surgical sperm retrieval (such as TESE) may be an alternative. Multiple samples can be collected on different days to maximize the total amount of sperm stored.

What to be aware of: Difficulty producing a sample on the day of collection is not uncommon, particularly under stressful circumstances. The clinic can offer accommodations and alternatives if needed. Sperm quality can also vary between samples, which is one reason multiple collections may be recommended.

The sample is analyzed for count, motility, and morphology. It is then divided into multiple vials and frozen using a controlled-rate freezing process or vitrification to preserve viability. The frozen samples are stored in liquid nitrogen at -196 degrees Celsius, where they can remain viable indefinitely. Storage consent forms are completed and ongoing storage arrangements are established.

What to be aware of: Not all sperm survive the freezing and thawing process. Post-thaw sperm quality is typically lower than a fresh sample - this is expected and is taken into account when planning future treatment. Storing multiple vials from several collections provides more flexibility for future use.

The frozen sperm remains in storage until needed. When fertility treatment is desired, the clinic is contacted to discuss the best approach based on the post-thaw sperm quality and the overall clinical situation. Depending on the quality, the sperm may be used for IUI, IVF, or IVF with ICSI.

paths.title: Egg Freezing
paths.description: Preserve fertility by freezing unfertilized eggs for future use
paths.suitable_for: Elective fertility preservation, before cancer treatment, before gonadotoxic medications, before gender-affirming hormone therapy, or medical conditions that may affect ovarian function

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and explain the egg freezing process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess ovarian reserve and guide the stimulation plan.

Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the stimulation and retrieval procedures, the freezing and storage process, and the terms of future use.
The clinical team selects the appropriate stimulation protocol based on age, ovarian reserve, and individual factors. Baseline ultrasound and bloodwork confirm readiness to begin.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound to track follicle growth and bloodwork to measure hormone levels. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.
When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Mature eggs are immediately frozen using vitrification, an ultra-rapid freezing technique that prevents ice crystal formation. The eggs are stored in liquid nitrogen at -196 degrees Celsius, where they can remain viable for many years. A report is provided on the number of mature eggs successfully frozen.

What to be aware of: Only mature eggs can be frozen. Some of the eggs retrieved may not have reached full maturity, so the number of eggs frozen may be lower than the total number retrieved. In some cases, more than one egg freezing cycle may be recommended to reach a sufficient number of stored eggs.

Storage consent forms are completed and ongoing storage arrangements are established. The frozen eggs remain in storage until needed. When fertility treatment is desired, the clinic is contacted to plan the next steps, which involve thawing the eggs, fertilizing them with sperm (using ICSI), culturing the resulting embryos, and transferring an embryo to the uterus.

paths.title: Embryo Freezing With Partner
paths.description: Create and freeze embryos using partner sperm for future use
paths.suitable_for: Elective fertility preservation, before cancer treatment, before gonadotoxic medications, embryo banking, or planning to delay pregnancy

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and explain the embryo freezing process. Required fertility testing is completed for both partners to guide treatment planning.

Required infectious disease screening is completed for both partners and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the stimulation, retrieval, fertilization, freezing, and storage procedures, as well as embryo disposition decisions.

The clinical team selects the appropriate stimulation protocol based on age, ovarian reserve, and individual factors. Baseline ultrasound and bloodwork confirm readiness to begin. Instructions for administering fertility medications are provided.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound to track follicle growth and bloodwork to measure hormone levels. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.
When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

A sperm sample is collected from the partner and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized using conventional insemination or ICSI, depending on the clinical situation. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs have fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process. In uncommon cases, fertilization may not occur, and the clinical team will discuss possible reasons and next steps.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

Embryos are frozen using vitrification, most commonly at the blastocyst stage (Day 5 or 6), though earlier-stage freezing may be used in certain situations. The frozen embryos are stored in liquid nitrogen where they can remain viable indefinitely. Storage consent forms are completed, including disposition decisions that specify what should happen to the embryos under various circumstances (such as separation, death, or decision to discontinue storage). When fertility treatment is desired, the clinic is contacted to plan a frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycle.

What to be aware of: The final number of embryos frozen reflects the natural selection that occurs during the culture period. Each embryo that reaches the freezing stage has demonstrated developmental competence. In some cases, more than one cycle may be recommended to accumulate a sufficient number of frozen embryos.

paths.title: Embryo Freezing With Donor Sperm
paths.description: Create and freeze embryos using donor sperm for future use
paths.suitable_for: Single individuals, same-sex couples, absence of a sperm-providing partner, genetic concerns requiring donor sperm, or elective fertility preservation

steps.overview: The first step is an initial consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and explain the embryo freezing with donor sperm process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess ovarian reserve and guide treatment planning.

Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the stimulation, retrieval, fertilization, freezing, and storage procedures, as well as embryo disposition decisions.

A sperm donor is selected from a donor bank. Profiles typically include information about physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor sperm.

The clinical team selects the appropriate stimulation protocol based on age, ovarian reserve, and individual factors. Baseline ultrasound and bloodwork confirm readiness to begin. Instructions for administering fertility medications are provided.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound to track follicle growth and bloodwork to measure hormone levels. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.
When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized using conventional insemination or ICSI, depending on the post-thaw sperm quality and the clinical team's recommendation. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs have fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Frozen donor sperm may have reduced motility after thawing, which the laboratory accounts for when selecting the fertilization method. Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

Embryos are frozen using vitrification, most commonly at the blastocyst stage (Day 5 or 6), though earlier-stage freezing may be used in certain situations. The frozen embryos are stored in liquid nitrogen where they can remain viable indefinitely. Storage consent forms are completed, including disposition decisions that specify what should happen to the embryos under various circumstances. When fertility treatment is desired, the clinic is contacted to plan a frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycle.

What to be aware of: The final number of embryos frozen reflects the natural selection that occurs during the culture period. Each embryo that reaches the freezing stage has demonstrated developmental competence. In some cases, more than one cycle may be recommended to accumulate a sufficient number of frozen embryos.

paths.title: Ovarian Tissue Cryopreservation
paths.description: Preservation of ovarian tissue containing immature eggs for future use
paths.suitable_for: Prepubertal individuals, when there is insufficient time for an egg freezing cycle before gonadotoxic treatment, or when hormonal stimulation is contraindicated

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist (and, where applicable, the treating medical team) to review the clinical situation, explain the ovarian tissue cryopreservation process, and discuss realistic expectations. The urgency and timing are assessed based on the planned medical treatment.

Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. For prepubertal patients, consent involves the parent or guardian with age-appropriate involvement of the patient. The consent process covers the surgical procedure, tissue storage, and the current status of future-use options.

Under general anesthesia, a surgeon removes a portion of ovarian cortex (typically from one ovary) through laparoscopic surgery. The tissue contains thousands of immature follicles. The procedure is coordinated with the treatment timeline to avoid delaying necessary medical care.

What to be aware of: The tissue may or may not contain viable follicles capable of future use, and the techniques for using the stored tissue are still evolving.

The ovarian tissue is processed into small strips and frozen using controlled freezing protocols. The tissue is stored in liquid nitrogen at -196 degrees Celsius, where it can remain viable for many years. Storage consent forms are completed, including disposition decisions.

The tissue remains in storage until needed. When medical treatment is complete and fertility is desired, the clinic is contacted to discuss options. The primary approach is ovarian tissue transplantation, in which the thawed tissue is surgically placed back into the body to restore hormone production and the potential for natural conception or egg retrieval. The clinical team will advise on the best approach based on the individual situation and current advances in reproductive medicine.

paths.title: Anonymous Embryo Donation Cycle
paths.description: Using previously created embryos from anonymous donors
paths.suitable_for: Individuals or couples requiring donor embryos, including those with diminished ovarian reserve, repeated IVF failure, genetic concerns, or single individuals and same-sex couples

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and explain the embryo donation process. Required testing is completed to assess readiness to carry a pregnancy. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor embryos.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness and pattern are monitored by ultrasound. The timing of progesterone initiation determines the transfer date.

The clinical team selects the embryo for transfer and places it into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, the clinical team will discuss next steps.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone and estrogen support continue during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled and medications typically continue through the first trimester before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available.

paths.title: Directed Embryo Donation Cycle
paths.description: Using previously created embryos from a known (directed) donor
paths.suitable_for: Individuals or couples receiving donated embryos from someone known to them, such as family members, friends, or other patients

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and explain the directed embryo donation process. Required testing is completed for the recipient to assess readiness to carry a pregnancy. The known donors must also complete infectious disease screening. Legal agreements between the donor(s) and recipient(s) are established to define parental rights and responsibilities. Counseling is provided for all parties to address the emotional and relational aspects of known donation.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness and pattern are monitored by ultrasound. The timing of progesterone initiation determines the transfer date.

The clinical team selects the embryo for transfer and places it into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, the clinical team will discuss next steps.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone and estrogen support continue during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled and medications typically continue through the first trimester before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available.

paths.title: Frozen Egg IVF - Partner Sperm with ICSI
paths.description: IVF using previously frozen eggs with partner sperm. ICSI is used for fertilization due to changes in the zona pellucida from the freezing process.
paths.suitable_for: Individuals using their own previously frozen eggs with partner sperm

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and plan the cycle for using the frozen eggs. Required testing is completed to confirm readiness for treatment.

Required infectious disease screening is completed (or confirmed to be current) and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the egg thawing, fertilization, embryo culture, and transfer procedures.

The frozen eggs are thawed using a controlled warming protocol. A sperm sample is collected from the partner and prepared in the laboratory. ICSI is used to fertilize the thawed eggs, as the freezing process causes changes to the zona pellucida that make conventional insemination less effective. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs survived thawing and fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all frozen eggs will survive the thawing process, and not all surviving eggs will fertilize. This is a normal part of the process and is accounted for in treatment planning.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness and pattern are monitored by ultrasound. The timing of endometrial preparation is coordinated with the embryo development timeline - depending on the protocol, preparation may begin before egg thawing so the lining is ready when embryos reach the transfer stage, or embryos may be frozen after culture and transferred in a separate prepared cycle.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone and estrogen support continue during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Frozen Egg IVF - Donor Sperm with ICSI
paths.description: IVF using previously frozen eggs with donor sperm. ICSI is used for fertilization due to changes in the zona pellucida from the freezing process.
paths.suitable_for: Single individuals, those with severe sperm factor infertility, or absence of a sperm-providing partner, using their own previously frozen eggs

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and plan the cycle for using the frozen eggs with donor sperm. Required testing is completed to confirm readiness for treatment.

Required infectious disease screening is completed (or confirmed to be current) and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the egg thawing, fertilization, embryo culture, transfer procedures, and the use of donor sperm.

A sperm donor is selected from a donor bank. Profiles typically include information about physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor sperm.

The frozen eggs are thawed using a controlled warming protocol. Donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. ICSI is used to fertilize the thawed eggs, as the freezing process causes changes to the zona pellucida that make conventional insemination less effective. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs survived thawing and fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all frozen eggs will survive the thawing process. Frozen donor sperm may also have reduced motility after thawing. Not all surviving eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process and is accounted for in treatment planning.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness and pattern are monitored by ultrasound. The timing of endometrial preparation is coordinated with the embryo development timeline - depending on the protocol, preparation may begin before egg thawing, or embryos may be frozen after culture and transferred in a separate prepared cycle.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone and estrogen support continue during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Donor Egg IVF with ICSI
paths.description: IVF using frozen donor eggs with ICSI fertilization
paths.suitable_for: Diminished ovarian reserve, advanced reproductive age, premature ovarian insufficiency, genetic concerns, or repeated IVF failure with own eggs

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals, and explain the donor egg IVF process. Required testing is completed to assess readiness to carry a pregnancy.

Required infectious disease screening is completed and legal contracts are established to define parental rights. The clinic coordinates the preparation of frozen donor eggs for the cycle. Counseling is provided to address the emotional, legal, and practical aspects of using donor eggs.
An egg donor is selected from a donor egg bank. Profiles typically include physical characteristics, medical history, genetic testing results, and educational background.
The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications to create a receptive environment for embryo implantation. Lining thickness and pattern are monitored by ultrasound.

Donor eggs are thawed using a controlled warming protocol. Sperm is collected from the partner or donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. ICSI is used to fertilize the eggs, as the freezing process causes changes to the zona pellucida that make conventional insemination less effective. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs survived thawing and fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all frozen eggs will survive the thawing process, and not all surviving eggs will fertilize. This is a normal part of the process and is accounted for in treatment planning.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Progesterone support continues after transfer. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone and estrogen support continue during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Sperm Freezing (Non-Cancer)
paths.description: Preserve fertility by banking sperm for future use - for non-oncological medical reasons, before vasectomy, or when future sperm availability may be uncertain
paths.suitable_for: Before vasectomy, before gender-affirming hormone therapy, before gonadotoxic medications, military deployment or extended travel, declining sperm quality, or when future availability may be uncertain

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist or reproductive urologist to review medical history, discuss the reason for preservation, and explain the sperm freezing process. A semen analysis may be performed to establish baseline sperm parameters.
Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the collection, freezing, storage, and future use of the samples.

A semen sample is collected, typically by ejaculation in a private collection room at the clinic. Some clinics allow home collection with specific transport and timing requirements. If ejaculation is not possible, surgical sperm retrieval (such as TESE) may be an alternative. Multiple samples can be collected on different days to maximize the total amount of sperm stored.

What to be aware of: Difficulty producing a sample on the day of collection is not uncommon, particularly under stressful circumstances. The clinic can offer accommodations and alternatives if needed. Sperm quality can also vary between samples, which is one reason multiple collections may be recommended.

The sample is analyzed for count, motility, and morphology. It is then divided into multiple vials and frozen using a controlled-rate freezing process or vitrification to preserve viability. The frozen samples are stored in liquid nitrogen at -196 degrees Celsius, where they can remain viable indefinitely. Storage consent forms are completed and ongoing storage arrangements are established.

What to be aware of: Not all sperm survive the freezing and thawing process. Post-thaw sperm quality is typically lower than a fresh sample - this is expected and is taken into account when planning future treatment. Storing multiple vials from several collections provides more flexibility for future use.

The frozen sperm remains in storage until needed. When fertility treatment is desired, the clinic is contacted to discuss the best approach based on the post-thaw sperm quality and the overall clinical situation. Depending on the quality, the sperm may be used for IUI, IVF, or IVF with ICSI.

paths.title: Elective Egg Freezing
paths.description: Preserve fertility by freezing eggs for future use - for personal, career, educational, or other elective reasons
paths.suitable_for: Individuals who wish to preserve fertility for future use when pregnancy is not being pursued at this time

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss goals and timeline, and explain the egg freezing process. Required fertility testing is completed to assess ovarian reserve and guide the stimulation plan.

Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the stimulation and retrieval procedures, the freezing and storage process, and the terms of future use.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound to track follicle growth and bloodwork to measure hormone levels. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.
When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Mature eggs are immediately frozen using vitrification, an ultra-rapid freezing technique that prevents ice crystal formation. The eggs are stored in liquid nitrogen at -196 degrees Celsius, where they can remain viable for many years. A report is provided on the number of mature eggs successfully frozen.

What to be aware of: Only mature eggs can be frozen. Some of the eggs retrieved may not have reached full maturity, so the number of eggs frozen may be lower than the total number retrieved. In some cases, more than one egg freezing cycle may be recommended to reach a sufficient number of stored eggs.

Storage consent forms are completed and ongoing storage arrangements are established. The frozen eggs remain in storage until needed. When fertility treatment is desired, the clinic is contacted to plan the next steps, which involve thawing the eggs, fertilizing them with sperm (using ICSI), culturing the resulting embryos, and transferring an embryo to the uterus.

paths.title: Excess Embryo Freezing
paths.description: Cryopreservation of additional good-quality embryos from an IVF cycle for future use
paths.suitable_for: Individuals or couples with good-quality embryos remaining after fresh embryo transfer, or as part of an embryo banking strategy

steps.overview: A consultation with the fertility specialist confirms the plan for freezing additional embryos from the current cycle. The number and quality of embryos available for cryopreservation are reviewed.
Informed consent documents are signed covering the freezing process, storage terms, and embryo disposition decisions - including what should happen to the embryos under various future circumstances.

Embryos are frozen using vitrification, most commonly at the blastocyst stage (Day 5 or 6), though earlier-stage freezing may be used in certain situations. The frozen embryos are stored in liquid nitrogen where they can remain viable indefinitely.

What to be aware of: Not all embryos from the cycle will reach a stage or quality suitable for freezing. The embryology team assesses each embryo individually, and only those that have demonstrated adequate developmental potential are selected for cryopreservation.
Ongoing storage arrangements are established. The frozen embryos remain in storage until needed. When a frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycle is desired, the clinic is contacted to plan the next steps.

paths.title: Embryo Freezing for Genetic Testing
paths.description: Freeze-all IVF cycle with embryo biopsy for preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-A, PGT-M, or PGT-SR). All embryos are frozen while awaiting genetic analysis results.
paths.suitable_for: Individuals or couples requiring genetic testing on embryos for chromosomal abnormalities, single gene disorders, or structural rearrangements before transfer

steps.overview: The first step is a consultation with a fertility specialist to review medical history, discuss the indication for genetic testing, and explain the freeze-all with PGT process. Genetic counseling is provided to discuss the type of testing, what results may look like, and how they will guide transfer decisions.
topic_refs: Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Structural Rearrangements (PGT-SR)

steps.overview: Required infectious disease screening is completed and informed consent documents are signed. The consent process covers the stimulation, retrieval, biopsy, freezing, genetic testing, and future transfer procedures.

Daily injectable fertility medications (gonadotropins) are administered to stimulate the ovaries to develop multiple follicles simultaneously. Monitoring appointments occur approximately every 2 to 3 days and include ultrasound and bloodwork. The medication dose may be adjusted based on the response.

What to be aware of: The ovaries may respond more or less than expected - a weaker response may result in fewer follicles, while a strong response may require dose adjustments to reduce the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation. This is why regular monitoring is important.
When the follicles reach the target size, a precisely timed trigger shot is administered to complete final egg maturation. Egg retrieval is scheduled approximately 36 hours later.

Under sedation, eggs are retrieved from the ovaries using a transvaginal ultrasound-guided needle. The procedure typically takes 15 to 30 minutes, followed by a recovery period of 1 to 2 hours.

What to be aware of: Not every follicle will contain a mature egg, so the number of eggs retrieved may be lower than the number of follicles seen on ultrasound. This is normal and expected.

Sperm is collected from the partner or donor sperm is thawed and prepared in the laboratory. Mature eggs are fertilized, typically using ICSI when PGT is planned, to avoid contamination from residual sperm on the zona pellucida. The following day, the embryology team checks fertilization results.

What to be aware of: Not all mature eggs will fertilize - this is a normal part of the process. In uncommon cases, fertilization may not occur, and the clinical team will discuss possible reasons and next steps.

The embryos are cultured to the blastocyst stage (Day 5 or 6). The embryology team monitors development and grades quality. At the blastocyst stage, a trophectoderm biopsy is performed - a small number of cells are carefully removed from the outer layer of each suitable embryo and sent to the genetics laboratory for analysis.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing before reaching the blastocyst stage. Only embryos that reach an appropriate stage of development can be biopsied. The number of embryos available for biopsy and freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.
topic_refs: Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Structural Rearrangements (PGT-SR)

steps.overview: All biopsied embryos are immediately frozen using vitrification to preserve their quality while the genetic testing laboratory analyzes the biopsy samples. Results typically take 1 to 2 weeks.

Storage consent and disposition forms are completed. Once genetic testing results are available, the clinical and genetic counseling teams review the findings and discuss which embryos are suitable for transfer. A frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycle is planned for the selected embryo.

What to be aware of: Genetic testing results may show that some or all embryos are abnormal (aneuploid) or carry the condition being tested for. In some cases, no embryos may be suitable for transfer. This can be an emotionally difficult outcome. The clinical and genetic counseling teams are available to discuss results, what they mean, and options moving forward - which may include another retrieval cycle, alternative approaches, or other family-building paths.

paths.title: Frozen Embryo Transfer (FET) Cycle
paths.description: Transfer of a previously frozen embryo. This is one of the most common IVF cycle types, used when embryos were frozen from a previous cycle or after genetic testing.
paths.suitable_for: Individuals with frozen embryos from a previous IVF cycle, those who completed a freeze-all cycle, or those who have received genetic testing results on frozen embryos

steps.overview: A consultation with the fertility specialist is scheduled to review the available frozen embryos, discuss the transfer plan, and update any relevant medical history. If genetic testing was performed, the results are reviewed and the embryo for transfer is selected.

The uterine lining is prepared for embryo implantation. Depending on the protocol, this may involve a natural cycle (monitoring ovulation), a modified natural cycle (with a trigger shot), or a medicated cycle (using estrogen and progesterone). Lining thickness and pattern are monitored by ultrasound.

Once the lining reaches adequate thickness and the hormonal environment is appropriate, the transfer date is scheduled. Specific instructions are provided regarding medication timing, particularly the start of progesterone, which determines the transfer day.

On the day of transfer, the embryo is thawed using a controlled warming protocol. The clinical team places the embryo into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated.

What to be aware of: Embryo survival after thawing is very high with modern vitrification techniques, but not guaranteed in every case. Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity and embryo quality. If pregnancy does not occur, additional frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle.

Progesterone and any other prescribed medications continue after transfer to support the uterine lining during the waiting period. This interval between transfer and pregnancy testing is typically approximately two weeks.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. If positive, medications continue and early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care. If negative, the clinical team discusses next steps, which may include transferring another frozen embryo or adjusting the treatment plan.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Using Your Frozen Eggs
paths.description: Using previously frozen eggs for IVF treatment. This pathway covers egg thawing, fertilization, embryo culture, and transfer.
paths.suitable_for: Individuals who previously froze eggs and are now ready to pursue pregnancy

steps.overview: A consultation with the fertility specialist is scheduled to review the frozen egg inventory, update medical history, and plan the treatment cycle. Any new testing that may be needed is identified.
The sperm source is determined - partner sperm or donor sperm. If donor sperm is being used, donor selection and counseling are completed at this stage.

The frozen eggs are thawed using a controlled warming protocol. ICSI is used for fertilization, as changes to the zona pellucida during freezing make conventional insemination less effective. The following day, the embryology team checks how many eggs survived thawing and fertilized successfully.

What to be aware of: Not all frozen eggs will survive the thawing process, and not all surviving eggs will fertilize. This is a normal part of the process and is accounted for in treatment planning.

The fertilized eggs (now embryos) are cultured in the laboratory for 3 to 6 days. The embryology team monitors their development and grades their quality. Genetic testing (PGT) may be discussed and performed at this stage if indicated.

What to be aware of: It is normal for some embryos to stop developing during this period. This natural attrition means the number of embryos available for transfer or freezing may be lower than the number that initially fertilized.

The uterine lining is prepared using estrogen and progesterone medications. Once the lining is ready, the clinical team selects the highest-quality embryo for transfer into the uterus using a thin catheter guided by ultrasound. The procedure is brief and generally well tolerated. Any additional good-quality embryos can be frozen for future use.

What to be aware of: Transfer does not guarantee pregnancy - implantation depends on multiple factors including uterine receptivity. If pregnancy does not occur, frozen embryos can be used in a subsequent cycle without repeating the full process.

Approximately 9 to 14 days after transfer, a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Progesterone and estrogen support continue during this waiting period. If pregnancy is confirmed, early monitoring ultrasounds are scheduled before transitioning to routine prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available. If frozen embryos remain, a subsequent transfer can often be planned.

paths.title: Using Your Frozen Sperm
paths.description: Using previously frozen sperm for fertility treatment. This pathway covers coordinating the thaw with the appropriate treatment cycle (IUI or IVF).
paths.suitable_for: Individuals with previously frozen sperm who are ready to use it for fertility treatment

steps.overview: A consultation with the fertility specialist is scheduled to review the frozen sperm inventory, discuss the post-thaw quality expectations, and plan the treatment approach. Medical history is updated for all parties involved.

Based on the expected post-thaw sperm quality and the overall clinical situation, the treatment approach is determined. If sperm counts and motility are expected to be adequate after thawing, IUI may be appropriate. If post-thaw quality is limited, IVF with ICSI may be recommended.

The treatment cycle is coordinated - either cycle monitoring and ovulation timing for IUI, or ovarian stimulation and egg retrieval for IVF. The approach depends on the treatment plan determined in the previous step. If a gestational carrier is involved, their endometrial preparation is coordinated.

On the day of the procedure (IUI or egg retrieval), the frozen sperm sample is thawed and prepared in the laboratory using standard washing and concentration techniques.

What to be aware of: Post-thaw sperm quality is typically lower than a fresh sample. The laboratory assesses the thawed sample and confirms it is adequate for the planned procedure. In rare cases, if the post-thaw quality is insufficient for IUI, the clinical team may recommend switching to IVF with ICSI.

For IUI: the prepared sperm is placed directly into the uterus through a thin catheter. For IVF: the sperm is used to fertilize the retrieved eggs, typically using ICSI if the post-thaw count is limited. The embryology team confirms fertilization results the following day.

What to be aware of: For IUI, the chance of success per cycle is lower than with IVF - multiple cycles may be needed. For IVF, not all eggs will fertilize, which is a normal part of the process. The clinical team will discuss results and next steps after each procedure.

Approximately two weeks after the procedure (IUI) or embryo transfer (IVF), a blood pregnancy test (serum hCG) is performed. Based on the results, the next steps are discussed - either continuing with additional cycles or transitioning to prenatal care.

What to be aware of: A negative result can be emotionally difficult. The clinical team is available to discuss next steps, and support resources including counseling are available.
